originalTitle: Oscar Wilde: "We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars."

quote.author: Oscar Wilde
quote.text: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.

A1.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
A1.text: Oscar Wilde was a writer from Ireland. He wrote this line. The gutter is a dirty place on the street. So life can be hard for everyone. But some people still look up at the stars. This means: they still hope. They still dream of good things. Life is hard, but you can dream.

vocabulary.definition: a person who writes books
the dirty edge of a street where water goes
a small bright light in the night sky
to want something good to happen
to think about a nice future

A2.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
A2.text: Oscar Wilde was a famous Irish writer. In this line, the "gutter" is the dirty part of a street — it means a hard or low place in life. Wilde says that all of us are in the gutter: everyone has difficult times. But some people still look up at the stars. The stars mean hope, dreams, and beautiful things. So the message is simple and warm: even when life is hard, we can still choose to dream and to see something beautiful above us.

vocabulary.definition: known by many people
the dirty edge of a road; here, a hard place in life
not easy
the feeling that good things can happen
to decide to do one thing

B1.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
B1.text: Oscar Wilde was a celebrated Irish writer of the late 1800s, known for his sharp wit. This famous line comes from his play Lady Windermere's Fan. The "gutter" — the dirty channel at the edge of a street — stands for the hard, low, or disappointing side of life that everyone shares. Wilde's clever point is in the contrast: although we are all stuck in the same difficult reality, some people still lift their eyes to the stars. The stars represent hope, imagination, and higher dreams. In other words, our situation may be the same, but our attitude is a choice — and it is that choice to keep looking up that makes all the difference.

vocabulary.definition: famous and admired
clever and funny use of words
to represent or mean something
a clear difference between two things
the way you think and feel about life

B2.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
B2.text: Oscar Wilde, the Irish writer famous for his glittering wit, gave this line to a character in his 1892 play Lady Windermere's Fan. Its power lies in a single, vivid contrast. The "gutter" — the filthy channel that runs along the edge of a road — represents the grim, ordinary, or disappointing conditions that no one entirely escapes. Yet within that shared hardship, Wilde notes, some people still tilt their heads back and gaze at the stars: symbols of hope, beauty, and aspiration. The sentence works because it refuses easy despair without pretending the gutter isn't real. It quietly suggests that circumstances alone do not define us; what distinguishes one person from another is the direction of their gaze — whether they fix their eyes on the mud beneath them or the light above.

vocabulary.definition: brilliant and impressive
producing a strong, clear picture in the mind
unpleasant and depressing
a strong hope or ambition
to make one thing different from another
a long, steady look

C1.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
C1.text: Few epigrams capture Oscar Wilde's genius for compressing wisdom into wit as neatly as this line from Lady Windermere's Fan (1892). Its force comes from a deliberate collision of images. The gutter — that grubby trough at the roadside where rainwater and refuse collect — is Wilde's emblem for the mean, the mundane, the disappointing circumstances that are, he implies, our common lot; none of us is exempt. And yet, poised against that squalor, is the upturned face contemplating the stars, those age-old figures of aspiration, beauty and transcendence. What makes the aphorism endure is its refusal of two easy consolations at once: it will not sentimentally deny the gutter, nor will it surrender to it. Instead it locates human dignity not in our situation, which is shared and often shabby, but in the direction of our attention — in the stubborn, quietly heroic act of looking up.

vocabulary.definition: a short, clever, memorable saying
dull and ordinary
a symbol that stands for an idea
a dirty, wretched condition
a short saying that states a truth
rising above ordinary limits

C2.title: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
C2.text: It is characteristic of Oscar Wilde that his most consoling remark should arrive dressed as a joke. Spoken in Lady Windermere's Fan (1892), the line yokes together two images with a jeweller's precision: the gutter and the stars, the basest thing underfoot and the loftiest thing overhead. The gutter is Wilde's shorthand for the shared indignity of existence — the drab, the compromised, the faintly sordid conditions from which no life is wholly spared. Against it he sets not escape but posture: the mere, defiant tilt of the head. Crucially, the epigram democratises misfortune while individualising hope. We are all, without exception, in the gutter; that much is decreed. But the stars are optional, a matter of where one chooses to point one's gaze — and it is in that small, unforced choice, rather than in any change of circumstance, that Wilde locates whatever nobility we are capable of. The wit disarms the sentiment, and the sentiment survives the wit; which is, more or less, the whole secret of his style.

vocabulary.definition: to join two things closely
low; without moral value
dirty and morally low
a way of holding the body; here, an attitude
to order officially; here, to fix as fate
to remove someone's suspicion or hostility
